front: Poem: opens 'Let us live, my Lesbia, and let us love' (Vivamus, mea Lesbia, atque amemus)
Poem: speaker asks for 'a thousand kisses, then a hundred' — 3300 kisses total
Poem: addressee told to value 'rumors of old men' at 'just one penny'
Poem: 'when our brief light has set, night is one long everlasting sleep'
Poem: instructs the addressee to 'confuse them so as not to know them all, / so that no enemy may cast an evil eye' by knowing the number of kisses
Poem: uses 'the stars in the tacit night' and 'the sandgrains in the desert' to measure affection
Poem: ends with 'ave atque vale' — 'hail and farewell'
Poem: elegy for the author's dead brother
Poem: speaker 'carried through many nations and over many seas' to 'wretched funeral rites'
Poem: Anne Carson's Nox (an accordion-format poetry box) draws on it — word-by-word translation renders first line as 'Many the peoples many the oceans I crossed'
Poem: gifts 'dripping much with fraternal weeping' placed on grave; addresses 'silent ashes'
Poem: Swinburne titled his elegy for Baudelaire 'Ave Atque Vale' in reference to this poem
Poem: written in elegiac couplets (hexameter + pentameter)
Poem: 'Fortune has taken your own self away from me'
Poem: final line begins 'atque in perpetuum' ('and for eternity')
Poem: Aeneas quotes from it in Book XI of the Aeneid while lamenting Pallas
Poem: Catullus's longest poem — an epyllion in dactylic hexameter about the wedding of Peleus and Thetis
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: extended ekphrasis on a bedspread/coverlet depicting Ariadne abandoned by Theseus
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: opens with 'pine trees formerly grown on Mt. Pelion's summit' swimming through Neptune's waters — describing the Argo
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: ends with gods refusing to visit humanity, 'nor endure the touch of clear daylight' — marking the onset of the Iron Age
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: Three Fates sing a gloomy epithalamium with the repeated line 'fly, guiding threads, fly spindle,' predicting Achilles's birth, life, and death
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: Ariadne compared to a 'Bacchante's stone statue' with exposed 'milk-white breasts'
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: Ariosto drew on it for his depiction of Bireno abandoning Olympia in Orlando Furioso
work: Carmen 64 (The Wedding of Peleus and Thetis)

front: Poem: opens with threat to 'sodomize and face-fuck' Furius and Aurelius
Poem: addressees think the narrator 'less of a man' after reading about his 'countless kisses'
Poem: narrator says his poems have wit and charm to arouse 'hairy old men who can't get it up'
Poem: written in hendecasyllabic meter; Kenneth Quinn called it the prime example of urbanitas wit
Poem: NPR bleeped out even the Latin version in a 2009 discussion with Mary Beard
Poem: Thomas Nelson Winter said this poem's history is 'the history of two of its fourteen lines'
Poem: mourns the death of Lesbia's pet sparrow, which was 'sweeter than honey'; curses 'evil Shades of Orcus' as 'destroyers of beauty'
Poem: describes a girl offering her fingertip to a pet sparrow
Poem: inspired Ovid's elegy on the death of Corinna's parrot
Poem: John Skelton's Phyllyp Sparowe pays homage to these poems
Poem: 'I hate and I love... I feel it happening and I am tortured' — a famous two-line poem
Poem: adaptation of Sappho 31 — 'He seems to me to be equal to a god' — in Sapphic stanzas
Poem: contains the line 'Leisure has destroyed kings and blessed cities'
Poem: speaker feels guilty for stealing 'a kiss more sweet than sweet ambrosia' from Juventius and feels 'crucified on the highest cross'
Poem: censors pretended the male addressee Juventius was a girl named Crastinia with a boyish nickname
Author: Lesbia, his lover in many poems, was likely Clodia Pulchra — sister of Publius Clodius Pulcher
Author: wrote 116 carmina; three were wrongly attributed in Marc-Antoine Muret's 1554 edition
Author: used hendecasyllabic meter in 43 of his poems
Author: translated Callimachus's Lock of Berenice into Latin; first poem asks 'to whom shall I dedicate this new slim little book?'
Author: Louis Zukofsky rendered a line from a galliambic poem as the homophonic nonsense 'the cat's cut out is cool rare air'
Author: Carmen 63 describes the priest Attis's self-mutilation in devotion to Cybele, in galliambic meter
Author: surviving works descend from a lost Verona manuscript; the O source is one of three directly descended manuscripts
Author: popularized in 17th-century France via Jean de la Chapelle's novel about his Adventures
Author: Yeats's 'The Scholars' concludes with the possibility of this poet 'walk[ing] that way!'
Author: Swinburne opens a poem addressed to this man by calling him 'my brother'
Author: Daisy Dunn wrote a fictional biography titled after this poet's bedspread
Author: Robinson Jeffers's 'Be Angry at the Sun' tells critics 'you are not [this poet] you know, / to lampoon these crude sketches of Caesar'
Poem: ends with image of a 'conscious blush' creeping over a 'downcast face' — an apple given by a lover falls from a girl's blouse when she jumps up to greet her mother
Poem: attacks a door for disloyalty; the door replies that a woman in the house had her virginity taken by her husband's father
Poem: describes another writer's volume as 'wrapping for mackerel,' contrasted with his friend's masterwork Smyrna
Poem: harangues a friend for stealing napkins; compares him to Fabullus and Veranius who 'does not use his left hand too well'
Poem: abuses an acquaintance for the caprine (goat-like) stench from his armpits
Poem: reviles a miser with 'body drier than bone' who believes 'sufficiency is riches' and has neither 'beetles nor spiders nor fire'
Poem: compares an estranged lover to a flower broken down by a plough; describes Britain as 'horribly distant'
